questions.to_find: The translation (postfix output) produced by the SDTS for the expression (a + b . c)

understand.plain: We have an expression grammar with two binary operators: + and . (dot). Each production has a semantic action that prints a symbol. An LR parser fires the semantic action right after each reduction. We need to trace the bottom-up parse of (a + b . c) and record what gets printed in order.

keywords.explain: A context-free grammar augmented with semantic actions (program fragments) attached to productions. Actions fire at specific points during parsing.
keywords.example: E -> E + T { print '+' } fires and prints '+' every time E -> E+T is reduced.

keywords.explain: In bottom-up (LR) parsing, semantic actions attached to the right-end of a production execute immediately after the reduction. This produces a postfix ordering of operators.
keywords.example: For a + b, first 'a' is shifted and reduced to id (printing 'a'), then 'b' is reduced (printing 'b'), then E+T is reduced (printing '+'), giving postfix: a b +

keywords.explain: An expression notation where operators follow their operands. LR bottom-up parsing with end-of-production actions naturally generates postfix.
keywords.example: a + b becomes a b + in postfix.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe understand.visual_alt: Parse tree for (a + b . c): root E reduces via E+T; the left E reduces via E.T which further has E(id:a) . T(id:b); the right T is id(c). LR bottom-up reductions print: a, b, dot, c, plus.

given.aim: Find the sequence of symbols printed by the SDTS when the LR parser parses (a + b . c)

terms.meaning: When the parser reduces E+T to E, it prints '+'
terms.example: Reducing a+b prints '+' after both operands are already printed
terms.connects: The '+' appears after operands in the output — postfix position

terms.meaning: When the parser reduces E.T to E, it prints '.'
terms.example: Reducing b.c prints '.' after b and c
terms.connects: The '.' appears after its operands in the output

terms.term: E -> id { print id.name } and T -> id { print id.name }
terms.meaning: When a bare identifier is reduced to E or T, its name is printed
terms.connects: Operands are printed first (leftmost), so all operators appear in postfix

given.plan: Simulate the LR parse of (a + b . c) step by step. At each reduction, fire the attached action. Collect the printed symbols in order to get the translation.

steps.title: Determine the parse structure
steps.formula_raw: E -> (E + T) where the left E is (E . T), left E of dot is id(a), T of dot is id(b), T of + is id(c)
steps.apply: The expression (a + b . c) is parsed as (a + (b . c)) if . binds tighter, or ((a + b) . c) depends on grammar precedence. Looking at the grammar: E -> E+T and E -> E.T — both have E on the left, T on the right. T -> id only (T cannot derive E.T), so . has higher precedence than +. Thus: a + (b . c) where b.c is the left-associative sub-expression. Parse tree root: E -> E + T, left E -> E . T (but wait — E.T means left E and right T; b is id->T->... let's trace exactly). Actually E -> E . T: left E must reduce from something; here a is id->E (via E->id), b is id->T (via T->id), c is id->T. So the parse is: E(a) . T(b) -> E, then E + T(c) -> E.
steps.note: In this grammar T->id so T cannot use the dot operator. The dot sub-expression is E . T = a . b, giving E, then E + T = (a.b) + c.

steps.title: Trace LR reductions and print actions
steps.formula_raw: Reduction order (bottom-up, left to right): id(a)->E, id(b)->T, E.T->E {print '.'}, id(c)->T, E+T->E {print '+'}
steps.apply:
Step-by-step:
1. Shift 'a', reduce id->E: print 'a'
2. Shift '.'
3. Shift 'b', reduce id->T: print 'b'
4. Reduce E . T -> E: print '.'
5. Shift '+'
6. Shift 'c', reduce id->T: print 'c'
7. Reduce E + T -> E: print '+'
Printed sequence: a b . c +
steps.note: The output is postfix notation. The dot (.) operator binds tighter than + because T->id only, so b.c cannot form; it is a.b that forms via E.T, and then +c. Result: a b . c +

solution.result: The translation (postfix output) for (a + b . c) is: a b . c +

questions.formula_note: SDTS with LR parser: actions at end of productions produce postfix translation. Each operator is printed after both operands are fully reduced.

questions.question:
Let the attribute 'val' give the value of a binary number generated by S in the following grammar:
  S -> L . L
  S -> L
  L -> L B
  L -> B
  B -> 0
  B -> 1
For example, an input 101.101 gives S.val = 5.625.
Construct a syntax directed translation scheme using only synthesized attributes, to determine S.val.
questions.to_find: A syntax-directed translation scheme using only synthesized attributes to compute S.val (value of the binary number)

understand.plain: We have a grammar that generates binary numbers with an optional decimal point (like 101.101). We need to write semantic rules (using only synthesized attributes — values computed from children) to compute the numeric value of such a binary number. The example 101.101 = 5 + 0.625 = 5.625.

keywords.explain: An attribute whose value is computed from the attribute values of the children of a node in the parse tree. Information flows up the tree.
keywords.example: In B -> 1 { B.val = 1 }, B.val is synthesized from the terminal 1.

keywords.explain: An extra synthesized attribute to track the number of binary digits in the string generated by L. This is needed to correctly scale the fractional part.
keywords.example: For '101', L.len = 3, so the fractional value is L.val / 2^3.

understand.visual_alt: Binary number grammar parse: 101.101 — integer part L generates 101 (value 5, length 3), fractional part L generates 101 (value 5, length 3), so S.val = 5 + 5/8 = 5.625.

given.aim: Compute the decimal value of a binary number (with possible fractional part) using only synthesized attributes

terms.meaning: Binary number with integer part (first L) and fractional part (second L)
Append digit B to the right of the string generated by L1
Base case: a single binary digit with its numeric value
terms.connects: Provides B.val to parent L rules

given.plan: Assign val and len as synthesized attributes to L and B. Build L.val left-recursively by shifting and adding digits. Use L.len to scale the fractional part when computing S.val.

steps.title: Base case rules for B and single-digit L
steps.formula_raw: B.val = digit value; L.val = B.val; L.len = 1
steps.note: L.len counts binary digits in the string generated by L, needed for fractional scaling.

steps.title: Recursive L rule
steps.apply:
L -> L1 B { L.val = L1.val * 2 + B.val; L.len = L1.len + 1 }
This works left-to-right: each new digit B is appended, shifting the existing value left (multiply by 2) and adding B.
steps.note: For '101': start with B=1 (L.val=1,len=1), then L1B with B=0 gives val=2,len=2, then L1B with B=1 gives val=5,len=3.

steps.title: S rules combining integer and fractional parts
steps.note: The fractional part L2 generates digits d1d2...dk; its decimal value is (L2.val)/2^k where k = L2.len.

questions.formula_note: Synthesized attributes val and len propagate up the parse tree; fractional binary value = integer_value / 2^(number_of_fractional_digits).

questions.answer:
A. E.val = -10; parse tree has root E, left subtree E1-T for (4-2), right T for (4*2). E.val = (4-2) - (4*2) = 2 - 8 = -6. Wait: left-associative: ((4-2)-4)*2 is wrong; E->E1-T->E1-T->num. Correct: 4-2-4*2 = ((4-2)-(4*2)) = 2-8 = -6.
B. Add attribute 'count' to E, T, F: each reduction increments count by summing children counts + 1.
questions.to_find: A. E.val for 4 - 2 - 4 * 2; B. Modified SDTS to count total number of reductions

understand.plain: Part A: We have a standard arithmetic expression grammar with +, -, * operators. We trace the parse of 4-2-4*2 and compute its value using the given rules. Part B: We need to add a 'count' attribute that accumulates the total number of reductions (each production firing counts as one reduction).

keywords.explain: The numeric value of the expression parsed by E, computed bottom-up from operand values and the operator.
keywords.example: E -> E1 + T { E.val = E1.val + T.val } computes sum at reduction time.

keywords.explain: Every time a production is applied (a string of symbols is reduced to a nonterminal), that is one reduction. Counting all reductions gives the total number of steps in the bottom-up parse.
keywords.example: Parsing 'num' to F is one reduction; F to T is another; T to E is a third.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='300' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='230' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Parse Tree: 4 - 2 - 4 * 2</text><text x='230' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E (val=-6)</text><line x1='230' y1='63' x2='130' y2='93' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='63' x2='230' y2='93' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='63' x2='340' y2='93' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E (val=2)</text><text x='230' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>-</text><text x='340' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T (val=8)</text><line x1='130' y1='115' x2='70' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='115' x2='130' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='115' x2='190' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>E(4)</text><text x='130' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>-</text><text x='190' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(2)</text><line x1='340' y1='115' x2='300' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='340' y1='115' x2='340' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='340' y1='115' x2='390' y2='145' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='300' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(4)</text><text x='340' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*</text><text x='390' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F(2)</text><text x='230' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>E.val = (4-2) - (4*2) = 2 - 8 = -6</text><text x='230' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Left-associative: - groups left; * higher precedence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree for 4-2-4*2: root E reduces E1-T where E1 is (4-2) via another E-T reduction, and T is (4*2) via T*F. E.val = 2 - 8 = -6.

given.aim: A. Compute E.val for 4-2-4*2; B. Extend SDTS to count total reductions

terms.meaning: Arithmetic expression with subtraction (left-associative) and multiplication (higher precedence)
terms.connects: Grammar E->E1-T gives left-associativity; T->T1*F gives higher * precedence

terms.meaning: Synthesized attributes holding the numeric value at each node
terms.connects: Values propagate upward via rules like E.val = E1.val - T.val

terms.meaning: Each time a production fires (reduces), we increment a counter. The total is accumulated as a synthesized attribute.
terms.example: F->num fires 4 times (for 4,2,4,2), each adds 1; total reductions for 4-2-4*2 = 11
terms.connects: Add count attribute: at each production, count = sum of children counts + 1

given.plan: Part A: Trace the parse tree for 4-2-4*2 applying the given rules to compute E.val. Part B: Introduce a 'cnt' synthesized attribute; each production sets it to 1 plus the sum of children's cnt values.

steps.title: Parse tree and E.val computation
steps.formula_raw: E -> E1 - T: E.val = E1.val - T.val; T -> T1 * F: T.val = T1.val * F.val
steps.apply:
Due to grammar structure: * has higher precedence (handled at T level), - is left-associative (E -> E1 - T).
4 - 2 - 4 * 2 is parsed as (4 - 2) - (4 * 2):
- F(4).val=4, T(4).val=4, E(4).val=4 [via F->num, T->F, E->T]
- F(2).val=2, T(2).val=2 [via F->num, T->F]
- E1=(4) - T(2) -> E.val = 4-2 = 2 [via E->E1-T]
- F(4).val=4, T1(4).val=4 [via F->num, T->F]
- F(2).val=2 [via F->num]
- T1(4) * F(2) -> T.val = 4*2 = 8 [via T->T1*F]
- E(val=2) - T(val=8) -> E.val = 2-8 = -6

steps.title: Part B: Add cnt attribute to count reductions
steps.note: For 4-2-4*2: each 'num' contributes F.cnt=1, plus T->F (4 times), T->T*F (1), E->T (1), E->E-T (2) = 4+4+1+1+2 = 12 reductions total (including all nodes).

solution.result: A. E.val = -6 (parse: ((4-2)-(4*2)) = 2-8 = -6). B. Add 'cnt' synthesized attribute: cnt = 1 + sum of children's cnt; each production gets +1 for the reduction itself.

questions.formula_note: Synthesized attribute cnt counts reductions bottom-up: every production fires exactly once per node, so cnt at each node = 1 + sum of children cnt. Left-associativity and operator precedence encoded in grammar structure.

questions.answer: C. A label, then the code for S, then the code for E, then a conditional goto back to the label
questions.to_find: The structure of 3-address code generated for 'repeat S until (i > 0)' using the given SDTS

understand.plain: A repeat-until loop executes the body (S1) at least once, then evaluates the condition (E), and loops back if the condition is false (0). The SDTS generates: a label at the start of S1, the code for S1, the code for E, and then a conditional branch back to the label if E.place == 0 (meaning condition not yet satisfied).

keywords.explain: An intermediate representation where each instruction has at most one operator and three addresses (operands/result). Used as IR in compilers.
keywords.example: t1 = i > 0 is a 3-address instruction: result t1, operator >, operands i and 0.

keywords.explain: newlabel() generates a fresh label for branching targets; gen() emits an instruction into the output code stream.
keywords.example: gen('L1:') emits the label L1; gen('if t1 == 0 goto L1') emits a conditional branch.

keywords.explain: Execute S1 first (unconditionally), then evaluate E; if E is false (0), go back and repeat. The loop runs at least once.
keywords.example: repeat { x = x+1 } until (x > 10): increments x until x > 10, always executing at least once.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='260' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='170' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3-Address Code Structure</text><rect x='30' y='45' width='280' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='170' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>L1:  (generated label)</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='280' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>code for S (body)</text><rect x='30' y='135' width='280' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>code for E  (condition: i > 0)</text><rect x='30' y='180' width='280' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='203' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if E.place == 0 goto L1</text><text x='170' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Loop back when condition is false (0)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 3-address code layout: label L1, code for S body, code for E condition, conditional branch 'if E==0 goto L1'. This matches option C.

given.aim: Identify the structure of 3-address code generated for repeat-until using the given SDTS

terms.meaning: A new label is created and emitted at the start of the loop body
terms.example: L1: marks the beginning of S1's code
terms.connects: This label is the target for the conditional back-branch at the end

terms.meaning: The body of the loop (S1) is executed first, followed by the condition expression (E)
terms.example: In repeat x:=x+1 until x>10: first x:=x+1 code, then code to compute x>10
terms.connects: Order in generated code: label, S1 body, E evaluation, conditional goto

terms.meaning: If the condition evaluates to 0 (false), branch back to the loop start
terms.example: if t1 == 0 goto L1 where t1 holds value of (i > 0)
terms.connects: When condition is false (not yet satisfied), repeat the loop

given.plan: Read the semantic actions of the SDTS in order: first a label is generated, then S1.code, then E.code, then a conditional goto back to the label. Match this to the options.

steps.title: Trace the SDTS actions in order
steps.formula_raw: S -> repeat S1 until E { t1=newtemp(); S1.begin=newlabel(); gen(S1.begin ':'); S1.code; E.code; gen(if E.place==0 goto S1.begin) }
steps.apply:
The actions execute in the order written:
1. t1 = newtemp() — allocate temp (used for E result)
2. S1.begin = newlabel() — create label e.g. L1
3. gen(S1.begin ':') — emit 'L1:'
4. S1.code — emit code for the loop body
5. E.code — emit code to evaluate condition
6. gen('if' E.place '==' '0' 'goto' S1.begin) — emit conditional branch back to L1
So the generated code is: L1: [body code] [condition code] if E==0 goto L1
steps.note: The condition check is at the bottom (repeat-until), so body always executes first.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply:
Option C: 'A label, then the code for S, then the code for E, then a conditional goto back to the label' — exactly matches our trace.
Option A: missing the label explicitly.
Option B: says goto 'after the repeat block' (wrong direction).
Option D: no syntax error here.
steps.note: The goto goes BACK to the label (repeat), not forward (that would be for while-do or if). Answer is C.

solution.result: C. A label, then the code for S, then the code for E, then a conditional goto back to the label.

questions.formula_note: Repeat-until 3-address code pattern: label at top of body, body code, condition evaluation, conditional back-branch. The condition goto is back to start (not forward), distinguishing repeat-until from while-do.
